quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Lorenz curve"?

options.text: P(t) = K/(1 + Ae^(-rt)) where A = (K-P₀)/P₀; produces an S-shaped (sigmoidal) curve
∫u dv = uv - ∫v du; derived from the product rule: d(uv) = u dv + v du
L(x) plots the cumulative fraction of total income earned by the bottom x fraction of earners; perfectly equal distribution gives L(x) = x
Numerical approximation: start at (x₀,y₀), step by yₙ₊₁ = yₙ + f(xₙ,yₙ)·Δx with step size Δx

quiz_questions.explanation: "Lorenz curve" is defined as: L(x) plots the cumulative fraction of total income earned by the bottom x fraction of earners; perfectly equal distribution gives L(x) = x. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Gini coefficient"?

options.text: Change of base: logₐ x = ln x / ln a, so d/dx[logₐ x] = 1/(x ln a)
At a critical point c: f' changes + to -: local max; f' changes - to +: local min; no sign change: neither
To find absolute extrema on [a,b]: 1) find critical points, 2) evaluate f at critical points and endpoints, 3) compare values
G = 2∫₀¹ [x - L(x)]dx; ranges from 0 (perfect equality) to 1 (perfect inequality); measures income distribution

quiz_questions.explanation: "Gini coefficient" is defined as: G = 2∫₀¹ [x - L(x)]dx; ranges from 0 (perfect equality) to 1 (perfect inequality); measures income distribution. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Consumer surplus"?

options.text: From a graph, check if the function approaches the same value from the left and right; if not, the two-sided limit does not exist
Use similar triangles to relate the heights and distances of an object and its shadow; differentiate to find shadow length rate
At a critical point c: f' changes + to -: local max; f' changes - to +: local min; no sign change: neither
∫₀^Q* [D(q) - p*]dq; the area between the demand curve and market price, representing consumer benefit

quiz_questions.explanation: "Consumer surplus" is defined as: ∫₀^Q* [D(q) - p*]dq; the area between the demand curve and market price, representing consumer benefit. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Producer surplus"?

options.text: If f'(x) = 0 for all x in (a,b), then f is constant on [a,b]; proven using MVT
Average value has the same units as f(x); the (1/(b-a)) factor divides out the length units
∫₀^Q* [p* - S(q)]dq; the area between market price and supply curve, representing producer benefit
State the integral being evaluated, show the antiderivative, substitute limits, and simplify; credit for setup even if computation errors occur

quiz_questions.explanation: "Producer surplus" is defined as: ∫₀^Q* [p* - S(q)]dq; the area between market price and supply curve, representing producer benefit. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Cardiac output"?

options.text: CO = amount of dye / ∫₀ᵀ c(t)dt; using the Stewart-Hamilton principle to measure blood flow from dye concentration curves
Left: sample at left endpoint; Right: sample at right endpoint; Midpoint: sample at center of each subinterval
The interval must include both endpoints; on open intervals (a,b), extrema may be approached but not attained
EVT guarantees extrema exist but does not tell us where; we use critical point analysis and endpoint evaluation to locate them

quiz_questions.explanation: "Cardiac output" is defined as: CO = amount of dye / ∫₀ᵀ c(t)dt; using the Stewart-Hamilton principle to measure blood flow from dye concentration curves. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "L(x) plots the cumulative fraction of total income earned by the bottom x fraction of earners; perfectly equal distribution gives L(x) = x"?

options.text: Lorenz curve
Sign chart of f''
Interval additivity
Key points to plot

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Lorenz curve".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "G = 2∫₀¹ [x - L(x)]dx; ranges from 0 (perfect equality) to 1 (perfect inequality); measures income distribution"?

options.text: Gini coefficient
Fractional exponent derivatives
Rectilinear motion
Displacement vs. distance

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Gini coefficient".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "∫₀^Q* [D(q) - p*]dq; the area between the demand curve and market price, representing consumer benefit"?

options.text: Consumer surplus
Separation process
Comparison with first derivative test
Tangent lines to implicit curves

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Consumer surplus".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "∫₀^Q* [p* - S(q)]dq; the area between market price and supply curve, representing producer benefit"?

options.text: Limit does not exist
Producer surplus
Direct substitution
Verification technique

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Producer surplus".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "CO = amount of dye / ∫₀ᵀ c(t)dt; using the Stewart-Hamilton principle to measure blood flow from dye concentration curves"?

options.text: Inflection point at P = K/2
Common quotient rule errors
Cardiac output
Net change interpretation

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Cardiac output".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about More Applications of Integration is accurate?

options.text: Second Derivative Test provides a quick classification of critical points using concavity.
More Applications of Integration surveys additional real-world uses.
Logarithmic Differentiation systematically uses properties of logarithms to simplify complex differentiation.
Success requires clear justification of setup, correct antiderivatives, appropriate use of technology, and precise interpretation of results in the given context.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes More Applications of Integration as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of More Applications of Integration?

options.text: Lorenz curves and Gini coefficients measure income inequality using the area between the Lorenz curve and the line of equality.
Area Between Curves computes the area enclosed between two functions.
These three rules, combined with basic derivatives, enable differentiation of virtually any function.
Logarithmic Differentiation systematically uses properties of logarithms to simplify complex differentiation.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes More Applications of Integration as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about More Applications of Integration?

options.text: Antiderivatives: Integration Strategies develops a systematic approach to choosing the right technique.
Antiderivatives and Indefinite Integrals introduce integration as the reverse of differentiation.
Consumer and producer surplus in economics use area between curves.
A slope field (direction field) displays short line segments at grid points (x,y), each with slope equal to f(x,y) from dy/dx = f(x,y).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes More Applications of Integration as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of More Applications of Integration?

options.text: Arc Length and Surface Area uses integration to measure curve length and rotational surface area.
Cardiac output is estimated by integrating dye dilution curves.
A Riemann sum divides [a,b] into n subintervals and approximates the area using rectangles: Σᵢf(xᵢ*)Δx.
Basic antiderivative rules: ∫xⁿdx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C (n ≠ -1), ∫eˣdx = eˣ + C, ∫(1/x)dx = ln|x| + C, ∫cos x dx = sin x + C, ∫sin x dx = -cos x + C.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes More Applications of Integration as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on More Applications of Integration?

options.text: The Mean Value Theorem for Integrals guarantees there exists a c in [a,b] where f(c) = f_avg—the function actually achieves its average value at some point.
Basic Derivative Rules provide shortcuts for finding derivatives without using the limit definition every time.
These diverse applications demonstrate that integration is a universal tool for accumulating continuously varying quantities.
The inflection point occurs at P = K/2, where the population is growing fastest.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes More Applications of Integration as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of More Applications of Integration?

options.text: Speedometer readings as instantaneous velocity: the derivative of position with respect to time at each moment, computed electronically in modern vehicles
Financial analysis: if an investment grows from $1000 to $1200 over a year, MVT guarantees the instantaneous growth rate was exactly 20% at some moment
Water tank problems: given inflow rate and outflow rate functions, compute water level at any time t and find when the tank is fullest
Government policy: Gini coefficients computed from income data guide tax policy and social welfare program design

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of More Applications of Integration.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of More Applications of Integration?

options.text: Average temperature: f_avg = (1/24)∫₀²⁴ T(t)dt gives the average temperature over a 24-hour period, used for energy consumption estimates
Water tank problems: given inflow rate and outflow rate functions, compute water level at any time t and find when the tank is fullest
Modeling the trajectory of a projectile with air resistance: the implicit equation relating position components requires combined chain rule and implicit differentiation
Market analysis: consumer and producer surplus calculations inform pricing strategy and measure economic efficiency

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of More Applications of Integration.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is More Applications of Integration applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Financial analysis: if an investment grows from $1000 to $1200 over a year, MVT guarantees the instantaneous growth rate was exactly 20% at some moment
Quality control: setting an acceptable error margin (ε) for a product property and determining the input precision (δ) needed in the manufacturing process
Medical diagnostics: cardiac output measurement via thermodilution uses integration of temperature-change curves over time
Finding the slope of a circle x² + y² = 25 at any point: dy/dx = -x/y, giving the tangent line direction for navigation and engineering applications

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of More Applications of Integration.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of More Applications of Integration?

options.text: Numerical integration in computer science: trapezoidal and Simpson's rules are programmed into calculators and software for approximating integrals when antiderivatives don't exist in closed form
Probability: the Cauchy distribution has CDF (1/π)arctan(x) + 1/2, obtained by integrating the probability density 1/(π(1+x²))
Population growth modeling: d/dt[P(t)] where P involves power functions helps ecologists predict changes in wildlife populations
Government policy: Gini coefficients computed from income data guide tax policy and social welfare program design

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of More Applications of Integration.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows More Applications of Integration in practice?

options.text: Structural engineering: the second derivative of a beam's deflection function indicates whether a critical point is a maximum deflection (sag) or minimum
Moore's Law: the roughly exponential growth of transistor density in microchips (doubling every ~2 years) eventually creating unforeseen limits in physics
Market analysis: consumer and producer surplus calculations inform pricing strategy and measure economic efficiency
Economics: ∫₀ᵀ (revenue rate - cost rate)dt = total profit over the period [0,T]

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of More Applications of Integration.
